title_en: Zakat and Sawm: Rulings on Almsgiving and Fasting
title_ar: الزكاة والصيام: أحكامهما في المذهب الحنبلي
content_en:
Zakat is the third pillar of Islam, an obligatory financial act of worship that purifies wealth and strengthens social bonds within the Muslim community. Allah says: 'Take from their wealth a charity by which you purify and sanctify them' (9:103). The Prophet (peace be upon him) explicitly mentioned zakat as one of the five pillars upon which Islam is built (al-Bukhari and Muslim).

In Zad al-Mustaqni', al-Hajjawi covers zakat across multiple categories of wealth. For gold and silver, the nisab (minimum threshold) is 85 grams of gold or 595 grams of silver, and the rate is 2.5% after a full lunar year (hawl) has elapsed. For trade goods, their value is assessed against the silver nisab and 2.5% of the value is due. For agricultural produce, the rate varies: 10% if rain-watered, 5% if artificially irrigated.

Zakat on livestock applies to camels, cattle, and sheep/goats when they are freely grazing (sa'imah) and reach the nisab. For camels: 5 camels trigger zakat, with detailed tables of rates as numbers increase. For cattle: the nisab is 30 head, with a one-year-old male (tabi') due for every 30, and a two-year-old female (musinnah) for every 40. For sheep and goats: 40 animals trigger zakat of one sheep.

The eight categories of zakat recipients are specified in the Quran (9:60): the poor (fuqara'), the indigent (masakin), zakat collectors ('amilun), those whose hearts are to be reconciled (mu'allafat al-qulub), slaves seeking their freedom (riqab), debtors (gharimun), those fighting in Allah's cause (fi sabilillah), and stranded travelers (ibn as-sabil).

Sawm (fasting) in Ramadan is the fourth pillar of Islam. Allah commands: 'O you who believe, fasting is prescribed for you as it was prescribed for those before you, so that you may attain taqwa' (2:183). The fast of Ramadan begins upon confirmed sighting of the new moon or completion of thirty days of Sha'ban.

The conditions for obligatory fasting are: Islam, sound intellect, puberty, ability, and residence (for the obligation — not for validity). The obligatory elements of the fast are intention (made the night before for Ramadan fasts) and abstaining from all nullifiers from true dawn until sunset.

Fast nullifiers in the Hanbali school include: eating and drinking intentionally, intentional vomiting, sexual intercourse, ejaculation caused by direct touch, cupping (hijamah) — a distinctive Hanbali position based on the hadith 'The cupper and the one cupped have broken their fast' (Ahmad, Abu Dawud) — intentional injection of nutritive substances, menstruation, and postnatal bleeding.

The fidyah (expiation) for one who cannot fast due to permanent illness or extreme old age is feeding one poor person for each day missed. Those who miss days due to illness or travel make them up (qada') after Ramadan. Deliberately breaking the Ramadan fast without a valid excuse incurs kaffarah: freeing a slave, or fasting sixty consecutive days, or feeding sixty poor people.
